blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This formula represents the total differential of a multivariable function.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. Thus:

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} + \Delta y \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}
blocks.description: This equation relates the change in $z$ to the changes in $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: The change in $z$ due to the change in $x$ can be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ changes in $x$; and that fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21). Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface (by keeping $x$ constant). The change of position is denoted by $\Delta x$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates the relationship between the points P and Q on a surface, showing how the movement in the $x$ direction affects the value of $z$ while $y$ remains constant.
